content.title: Designing Cozies — A User-Centered Approach to Maternity Fashion
content.summary: Led end-to-end UX/UI design for an emerging maternity fashion brand, delivering a 35% uplift in usability scores and 25% increase in checkout completion through research-driven sizing tools and intuitive navigation built for expectant mothers.

content.body: Expectant mothers face a unique set of pain points that most e-commerce experiences completely ignore. Sizing is confusing when your body is changing week to week, time constraints mean every extra tap costs a sale, and trust is fragile for a brand no one has heard of yet. The goal was to build an empathetic, accessible shopping experience that made women feel understood — not just sold to.

content.text: I analyzed app usage, forums, and pregnancy communities, then interviewed 3 expectant and 8 new mothers from diverse backgrounds. A survey validated the findings. Key insights:
content.label: Research Methods

items: 85% of women prioritized comfort and mobility over style during pregnancy and postpartum
Sizing confusion was directly causing 62% of cart abandonment
Time-constrained mothers needed to reach checkout in 3 taps or fewer
Sustainability influenced 78% of purchase decisions — more than the brand assumed

content.body: I built three detailed user archetypes — the anxiety-driven first-time mom, the efficiency-focused experienced mom, and the discovery-driven gift buyer — each mapped to jobs-to-be-done. From there I mapped 8 core user journeys, identified 14 friction points, and reduced checkout from 9 steps to 4. Information architecture was validated through card sorting with 25 users, which surfaced a key insight: organizing by trimester outperformed organizing by occasion.

images.alt: Cozies UI design mockup on iPhone

content.body: The most critical problem was sizing. A static size chart doesn't work for a body that changes week to week. I designed a dynamic sizing tool with trimester sliders and real-time visualization — sizing accuracy improved 42% in testing over the previous approach.

Every interaction was also designed for one hand. Most users were shopping on their phones, often literally holding a baby with the other arm. Thumb-friendly patterns and biometric checkout reduced mobile abandonment and pushed completion up 28%.

Trust was the third pillar. For an unknown brand, every moment of doubt is a lost sale. Progress indicators, expert validation badges, and material transparency were woven throughout — not as marketing decoration, but as genuine reassurance.

items: Sizing accuracy across a changing body → Interactive trimester sliders with fit predictor (accuracy +42%)
Mobile checkout abandonment → One-thumb gesture flows with biometric auth (completion +28%)
Stakeholder alignment → Weekly design critiques with live Figma prototypes (revision cycles −60%)
Accessibility for pregnancy limitations → Voice navigation and large touch targets (WCAG AA compliant)

content.text: "Alex's work transformed our MVP into a category-defining experience. The sizing innovation alone drove our highest conversion month ever." — CEO, Cozies

items: Empathy mapping over assumptions — research surfaced 3× more insights than stakeholder interviews alone
Prove it small first — a simple MVP sizing tool validated faster and de-risked the full AR investment
Design tokens and daily standups cut handoff friction by 70% — collaboration is a design problem too
Heatmaps caught thumb-zone issues in v2 that added 18% engagement — ship, measure, iterate
